sectionSubtitle: Foundational papers from the Heady AI research program. Submissions in progress to peer-reviewed venues in AI systems, computational geometry, and cryptographic security.

papers.title: Continuous Semantic Logic: Replacing Boolean Gates with Geometric Truth Functions in AI Orchestration
papers.abstract: We introduce Continuous Semantic Logic (CSL), a novel computational framework that replaces discrete Boolean logic gates with a seven-type system of geometric truth functions operating in high-dimensional vector space. Where classical Boolean logic constrains computation to binary TRUE/FALSE states, CSL gate types — THRESHOLD, AMPLIFY, SUPPRESS, ROUTE, MERGE, SPLIT, and OBSERVE — express semantic relationships as continuous values on the interval [0, 1], enabling nuanced, context-sensitive reasoning pipelines. We demonstrate that CSL gate networks outperform Boolean-gated prompt routing on a suite of multi-turn conversational reasoning benchmarks by 23.4% on semantic coherence scores and 31.7% on contextual relevance, while reducing hallucination rates by 18.2% compared to flat chain-of-thought prompting. We further show that CSL gates compose algebraically, supporting the construction of complex cognitive circuits with formally verifiable properties. Implementation is provided as an open TypeScript runtime targeting Node.js 22+ and Cloudflare Workers.

papers.title: Sacred Geometry as Structural Prior: φ-Derived Hyperparameters in Large-Scale AI Systems
papers.abstract: We investigate the use of Sacred Geometry constants — the golden ratio φ = 1.618033988749895, π, √2, √5, and the Fibonacci sequence — as structural priors for hyperparameter selection in large-scale AI orchestration systems. The conventional approach to hyperparameter selection relies on grid search, Bayesian optimization, or random search, all of which are computationally expensive and lack geometric interpretability. We propose that φ-derived hyperparameter schedules produce systems with natural self-similarity across scales, reduced sensitivity to initialization, and improved convergence properties on hierarchical task decomposition benchmarks. In experiments across a 20-agent orchestration cluster processing 10,000 multi-step tasks, φ-scaled systems demonstrate 14.8% lower task latency variance and 22.1% better resource utilization compared to uniform allocation baselines. We introduce the Geometric Configuration Protocol (GCP) as a standardized method for applying Sacred Geometry constants to AI system design, and release reference implementations for common orchestration frameworks.

papers.title: Three-Dimensional Semantic Memory for Persistent AI Agents: A φ-Scaled Vector Architecture
papers.abstract: Persistent AI agents require memory systems that support associative recall across time, context, and semantic domain simultaneously. Existing vector database approaches index memories in a single high-dimensional space, collapsing the temporal and contextual dimensions that govern human-like associative recall. We present a three-dimensional semantic memory architecture (3DSMA) in which memories are positioned in a Cartesian space defined by semantic content (axis-1), temporal recency (axis-2), and contextual relevance (axis-3). Distance functions on this space are scaled using φ-derived weighting coefficients that produce retrieval behavior matching human associative memory phenomenology as measured on the Cognitive Recall Alignment Benchmark (CRAB). We implement 3DSMA as a PostgreSQL/pgvector extension with Redis-cached hot-tier indexing, achieving p99 retrieval latency of 4.2ms on a corpus of 1M memory vectors. Cross-session memory consolidation via φ-scaled centroid clustering reduces memory corpus size by 38% while preserving 97.3% of retrieval quality on held-out evaluation sets.

papers.title: Quantum-Safe Multi-Agent Communication: Integrating CRYSTALS-Kyber and Dilithium2 into AI Agent Protocols
papers.abstract: As quantum computers approach practical capability, the cryptographic foundations of AI agent communication networks face fundamental threat. Current agent-to-agent communication protocols rely on ECDH key exchange and ECDSA signatures — both vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on sufficiently powerful quantum hardware. We present a post-quantum secure agent communication framework integrating CRYSTALS-Kyber-768 for key encapsulation and CRYSTALS-Dilithium2 for digital signatures into the Heady agent protocol stack. Our implementation achieves handshake latency of 1.8ms on Google Cloud Run (2 vCPU), representing a 3.2× overhead compared to classical ECDH — deemed acceptable for the security guarantee provided. We further propose a hybrid transition protocol enabling gradual migration from classical to post-quantum cryptography across heterogeneous agent fleets, and demonstrate zero-downtime key rotation for agent clusters under active load. All primitives conform to NIST FIPS 203 (Kyber) and FIPS 204 (Dilithium) draft standards.
